title: Performance
summary: 9084 Contract — complete performance, substantial performance, and time obligations.
learningObjectives: Explain when performance discharges contractual obligations.
Analyse time clauses: conditions vs warranties.
Apply cases: Re Moore, Poussard, Union Eagle.

simpleExplanation.title: Doing what was promised
simpleExplanation.summary: Full performance ends the contract — partial or late performance may still discharge if the term breached was not a condition.
steps: Substantial performance — minor defects (Bolton v Mahadeva).
Time — 'time is of the essence' if stated.
Tender of performance — ready and willing.

flashcards.back: When party performs **exactly** what was promised — primary method of discharge.
Nearly complete performance with minor defects — still entitled to payment minus cost to remedy defects (*Bolton v Mahadeva*).
Complete — exact; substantial — main obligations done but not perfect; **entire contracts** require complete performance.
If stated or implied, late performance is **repudiatory** (*Union Eagle v Golden Achievement* — 10 minutes late on property contract).
Offer to perform ready and willing — if creditor refuses, debtor **discharged** (must be able and willing at right time/place).
Wrong mode of delivery (pears in wrong boxes) — **entire contract**, no payment despite substantial value delivered.

flashcards.front: What is the general rule of performance established in *Cutter v Powell (1795)*?
flashcards.back: The strict rule that performance of an entire contract must be precise and exact. Failure to complete the entire obligation means the party who has partially performed cannot claim any payment.
An equitable doctrine allowing a party who has performed the main part of their obligation, with only minor defects, to claim the contract price, minus the cost of rectifying those defects.

flashcards.front: What was the key distinction between *Hoenig v Isaacs* and *Bolton v Mahadeva*?
flashcards.back: In *Hoenig*, defects were minor and the cost of cure was small relative to the contract price, so performance was substantial. In *Bolton*, the central heating was so defective it did not achieve its main purpose, and the cost of cure was a significant proportion of the price, so performance was not substantial.
A contract where obligations are separated into distinct parts, with payment due for each part as it is completed, rather than only upon completion of the whole contract.

flashcards.front: What is the legal effect if 'time is of the essence' in a contract?
flashcards.back: The time for performance is a condition of the contract. Any delay, however small, will be a repudiatory breach, allowing the innocent party to terminate the contract and claim damages.

sections.content: The General Rule: Complete and Exact Performance

The traditional common law position on performance is strict and uncompromising. The general rule is that performance of a contractual obligation must be precise and exact. If a party fails to perform their entire obligation, they are not entitled to any payment. This is because performance is treated as a 'condition precedent' to the other party's obligation to pay. The classic authority for this principle is *Cutter v Powell (1795)*, where a sailor who died part-way through a voyage was not entitled to any wages, as he had not completed the entire journey. This rule can lead to harsh outcomes, as it does not account for situations where a party has conferred a significant benefit despite minor shortcomings in their performance. Consequently, the courts of equity developed several exceptions to mitigate this harshness.

items: The general rule requires performance to be complete and exact.
Performance is a condition precedent to payment.
The key authority is *Cutter v Powell (1795)*.
Failure to perform the entire obligation means no payment can be claimed under the contract.

sections.content: Exception 1: The Doctrine of Substantial Performance

The most significant exception to the strict rule is the doctrine of substantial performance. This equitable doctrine allows a party who has performed the contract, but with minor defects, to claim the contract price, subject to a deduction for the cost of rectifying the defects. The key question is whether the performance received is substantially what was bargained for. In *Hoenig v Isaacs (1952)*, a decorator was owed £750 but there were minor defects costing £55 to fix. The court held performance was substantial, and he was entitled to £750 minus £55. This is contrasted with *Bolton v Mahadeva (1972)*, where a central heating system installation was so defective it did not work. The cost of repair was a significant proportion of the contract price, so performance was not deemed substantial, and the contractor could claim nothing.

items: Applies where performance is not precise but the main purpose of the contract is achieved.
The claimant can recover the contract price minus the 'cost of cure'.
Compare *Hoenig v Isaacs* (substantial) with *Bolton v Mahadeva* (not substantial).
The court considers the nature of the defects and the cost of rectification relative to the contract price.

sections.content: Another exception applies where the contract is not 'entire' but is 'divisible' or 'severable'. If the parties have structured their contract so that obligations are divided into separate stages or instalments, with payment due upon completion of each part, then the rule in *Cutter v Powell* does not apply to the whole contract. Instead, each part is treated as a separate mini-contract. For example, a building contract might specify payments upon completion of the foundations, the roof, and final plastering. Similarly, a contract for the delivery of goods by instalments may be severable. If one part is performed correctly, the party is entitled to payment for that part, even if subsequent parts are defective or not completed. This prevents the performing party from being left entirely unpaid for work already done.

items: Applies where the contract can be broken down into separate, distinct obligations.
Payment is due for each part of the contract that is successfully completed.
This avoids the harshness of the 'entire contract' rule.
Common in construction contracts (stage payments) and contracts for delivery by instalments.

sections.content: The time for performance can be a critical aspect of a contract. If the contract expressly states that 'time is of the essence', or it can be implied from the nature of the contract (e.g., for perishable goods), then the specified time is treated as a condition. Any delay constitutes a repudiatory breach, allowing the innocent party to terminate the contract and claim damages. If time is not initially of the essence, it can be made so by the innocent party serving a notice on the defaulting party, giving them a new, reasonable deadline to perform, as seen in *Charles Rickards Ltd v Oppenhaim (1950)*. If time is not of the essence, a delay is treated as a breach of warranty, entitling the innocent party to damages only, but not to terminate the contract.

items: If 'time is of the essence', the time for performance is a condition of the contract.
Breach of a time condition allows the innocent party to repudiate (terminate) the contract.
Time can be made of the essence expressly, by implication, or by notice.
If time is not of the essence, delay is a breach of warranty, only giving a right to damages.

sections.content: In a problem question, first identify if the contract is 'entire'. If so, apply the strict rule from *Cutter v Powell*. Then, systematically consider the exceptions. Is performance substantial? Use *Hoenig* and *Bolton* to argue both ways, comparing the cost of cure to the contract price. Is the contract divisible? Look for evidence of stage payments or instalments. This structured approach will ensure you cover all relevant legal principles.

sections.question: A builder contracts to install central heating for £560. Work is done but the hot water pipes leak slightly. The householder refuses to pay anything. Advise the builder.
sections.solution:
**Contract type:** **Entire contract** — single lump sum for complete installation.

**Performance:** Builder has **substantially performed** — heating installed and works, but minor defect (leaking pipes).

**Bolton v Mahadeva:** Substantial performance entitles builder to **contract price minus** cost to remedy defects — not zero payment.

**Defect cost:** Leaking pipes cost ~£50 to fix — builder entitled to **£510** (£560 − £50).

**Householder's refusal:** Wrong to withhold **entire** payment — may deduct remedy cost only.

**Conclusion:** Builder can sue for **£510** — substantial performance doctrine applies; defect does not destroy right to payment.

faq.q: If a builder does a terrible job, can they still claim payment under 'substantial performance'?
faq.a: No, probably not. The doctrine of substantial performance only applies to defects that are minor in nature. If the work is fundamentally defective and fails to achieve the main purpose of the contract (like the central heating in *Bolton v Mahadeva*), the court will rule that performance was not substantial. In this case, the builder would not be able to claim any payment under the contract.

faq.q: Does 'substantial performance' mean the innocent party has to accept and pay for faulty work?
faq.a: Not exactly. The doctrine is a balance. The innocent party must pay the contract price, but they are entitled to deduct the amount it would cost to fix the defects (the 'cost of cure'). This ensures they get what they bargained for in monetary terms and are not left out of pocket. The party in breach is paid for the benefit they have conferred, but they are held responsible for their defective performance.

faq.q: Can I always just pay for part of a contract if the other party only does part of the work?
faq.a: No, this is a common misconception. The default assumption in English law is that a contract is 'entire', meaning full payment is only due upon full performance (*Cutter v Powell*). You can only pay for a completed part if the contract is 'divisible' or 'severable' – meaning it was structured from the outset with separate, payable stages. Without evidence of this, partial performance of an entire contract entitles the performer to nothing.

comparisonTable.caption: Comparison of Complete vs. Substantial Performance

cells: Performance must be precise and exact, with no deviation from the contractual terms.
Performance is not exact but the main purpose of the contract has been achieved, with only minor defects.
Equitable doctrine developed to mitigate the harshness of the common law.
Entitled to the full contract price.
Entitled to the contract price minus a deduction for the cost of rectifying defects.
If performance is not complete on an entire contract, the performing party is entitled to nothing.
If performance is not substantial, it is treated as a failure to perform, and the party may claim nothing (as in *Bolton v Mahadeva*).
